speaker: interviewer
text: You headed back toward to Warsaw. What happened next in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I went back to Warsaw, I started to look after the family because my brothers hadn't been back.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned your mother was ill. Was she all right?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was very, very ill, cancer. And it was a very sad time. Mother passed away very soon after my coming back. But my brothers, two brothers, came before my mother passed away. And she was buried without any fuss because we couldn't do anything. Was just a normal funeral. Went there to the funeral, and it was a very sad time. I started to come more to my future wife, which we'd been friends from the childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does your future wife have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Leah, Luba. And we'd been starting to think about getting married but not telling anybody at the moment that we'd like to get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: From what age did--

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew-- I knew my wife, I knew from the age of about three, four, when she was three, four year old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd been a friend of her brother as a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, very nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: --very little kid. We went and I used to come to the place there. I used to come very often. And then we talked and it was already-- I'd been trying to bring food, to buy food from outside Warsaw because I knew people, the Polish people. And I used to bring food mostly for the two families, for the Landaus and for the Goodridges.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we decide in 1941, we decide to get married. And we asked the one to give us the OK, was Luba's sister, Esther, which was the oldest one there. And she approved of it. And then we spoke to her mother. I spoke to my father. And everybody approved to it, and we did have a wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a shul or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, at the rabbi's place, because her mother was very religious-- Mrs. Goodridge.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the rabbi's house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the rabbi's house. That was Rabbi Cohen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rabbi Co-han.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cohen. Cohen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Co-han.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cohen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Co-han.

speaker: interviewee
text: Co-han. Cohen. Cohen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was the chief rabbi of Warsaw at that time. But I knew him. I didn't. And very important thing during the wedding, what happened--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We'd been 15 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We'd been standing under the chuppah, as the normal Jewish wedding. That's was the-- that's what her mother did want too. And suddenly when he said some prayer, the light went off because we'd been in a room without windows, special the last room in our cover because he didn't want the noise or anything to be outside in case of the Germans falling into the yard or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we did have it in there, and the light went off. The rabbi said few words that lights should be on, but that doesn't mean that this is a bad omen, because the devil did it, not ourselves. So he did have a little speech of humors.

speaker: interviewee
text: We put some candles which he did have there. And that was that. And we went downstairs. And as soon as we went, the 15, downstairs, the Germans did have arrived on the street. And we start to run in all directions. And then we went to Luba's mother. She did prepare a dinner. I haven't seen a dinner like that in my life. That mean nothing on the table, practically, very little. But that was quite all right. A few pieces of challah, a piece in the plate which looks like a fish, looks like a fish.

speaker: interviewer
text: I hear.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very poorly done, because we'd been short. There was a shortage. We could see it's a shortage of food. And then when I decide to start to go outside Warsaw and try to get from the villages to buy food, definitely, it's the Goodridges and Landaus did help with the money because I didn't have any.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they helped me with the money. And I used to go out and buy the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far did you have to travel to-- how far did you have to travel to get the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I did travel 200, 150 kilometers, 100 kilometers. But about 200 kilometers, I used to travel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how--

speaker: interviewee
text: By car. As a Christian, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. But how much food were you able to bring in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a sack of food or sometimes I even did have delivered the food by the Poles, which I bought from them, by a hat. But I knew that I'm buying and I'm a buyer, so they did treat me quite well. Some knew that I am Jewish. Some did not. The more friendly knew that I am Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you paid ahead, they always delivered the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They delivered and everything was all right. I did never have a problem with them as far as delivering or getting from them or taking immediately delivery.

speaker: interviewer
text: I picked up on the fact that you said sometimes you went out of Warsaw as a non-Jew. Did you then have papers and a different name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I did not have any papers at the moment. I did have a clear, nothing with me. No. Because at the very beginning a little bit you could travel. You could travel. I say out because I'm used to a ghetto. That's why I say out. For me, out of home is out all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that wasn't closed in or something. We still traveled a little bit here and there. You could travel, with a risk because you're Jewish, but you could travel at the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you could travel, but were there along the way checkpoints that you had to show identification?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was checkpoint for different things. Checkpoint was for food or something. At the beginning, not so much. But then start more and more definitely to look into the Jewish-- then I start to get papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you able to supply the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you able to supply both families with food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, till the ghetto was closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was when? When was the ghetto closed?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was closed in-- I can't remember the date.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The ghetto was closed, not '42. It was closed 1940 or '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was established in 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: Established.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was sealed off a little bit later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: It was established in 1940 and sealed a bit later. When you say the ghetto was closed, how was it closed?

speaker: interviewee
text: The closed, with the what you call it? With the wall around the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a special wall built to enclose the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A special wall was built, and the bridge from one little ghetto to the bigger ghetto, a bridge to cross from around the Christian side. So we build a bridge. And at one stage, glass and broken glass was a gold mine if someone did have. The Germans suddenly decide to get glass and to put on the top of the wall.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Broken glass.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. So why was it a gold mine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because you could sell it to the Jewish organization, to the Jewish-- what is it, the [INAUDIBLE], what you call it--

speaker: interviewer
text: The [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish company sort of thing, [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish-- not company, but Jewish-- where Czerniakow was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Community.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish community center. Czerniakow was there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the leader. No, that was the time when you could sell the glass. Suddenly glass was a very important thing for the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Under what direction was the wall built around the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Direction of the [INAUDIBLE] we call in Jewish. I don't know the name for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: But Czerniakow was the leader. The Jewish community center.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Judenrat.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Judenrat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct, Judenrat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the ghetto was sealed, were all the Jews from all over Warsaw, and was it only the Jews of Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the moment, that was Jews of Warsaw and some which came from little places to be in the ghetto on their own accord, because they told they'd be safer in the Warsaw ghetto than they'd be outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once the Jews were in the ghetto, were they able to travel freely inside and outside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You did have to have a special-- what you call it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Pass?

speaker: interviewee
text: --cards and special, yes, to go outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who issued these cards?

speaker: interviewee
text: The cards, all the Germans or the Judenrat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Judenrat. And did the cards give the name and identity of the person?

speaker: interviewee
text: In some cases, yes. In some cases, no. How was it known? That when a group went out to clean bricks outside the ghetto, only the leader which was taking him out, the Jewish leader, he was only the one which got the card and then counting how many people are going out. The same was with the people which are taking out dead people to the cemetery, the same. A wagonette was with three people, one in front and two pushing it and the dead people in the cart. But the German count only one, two, three--

speaker: interviewer
text: Living.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the number of the cart. And that used to come back three. What kind of three, not important. And that, we used.

speaker: interviewer
text: You utilized that.

speaker: interviewee
text: We used it a lot, because why? Some went out to the outside--

speaker: interviewer
text: To the area inside.

speaker: interviewee
text: --from the one cemetery to another one will be in the border of Christian cemetery. And we used to jump one and another one would jump in to go to the ghetto, took over our place, and went inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: David--

speaker: interviewee
text: So we used it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. The checkpoints. Were they manned by Germans, Jews, or were there two types of checkpoints?

speaker: interviewee
text: By Germans, Jews, and Polish police.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the one checkpoint or in different checkpoints?

speaker: interviewee
text: In all checkpoints. That was normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a mixture of the three people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three people.

speaker: interviewer
text: A German, a Jew, and Pole.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who commanded that checkpoint?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the German.

speaker: interviewer
text: The German. That was in the confines on the inside of the wall or the outside of the wall of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was on the border of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: On the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: --of the opening where the gate was to go out from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you say the opening of the gate. Was there a curfew in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the curfew consisted of what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of from I can't-- I think that was from about 8:00 till about 8:00 or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jews worked outside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: How many?

speaker: interviewer
text: Jews worked outside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very hard to say. Quite a few hundred, because there was groups which was working and cleaning the bricks from the demolished houses, the bombed houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they always allowed outside to work or did that change?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That didn't change till the last minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We did work outside, because I used those groups to go out and to smuggle in arms and for our organization and food.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have twice said you used the groups for the organization. When three people went out carrying the dead bodies, there was a changeover of the people. Could you explain what you mean of a changeover of the person and how you used the groups? What did 'I used the groups' mean, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people which we changed is the people which have been on the Christian side. Doesn't mean that I've been in organization. They could be private people smuggling in food to the ghetto. But always we found them in the cemetery and boys who is staying here, the question--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is staying where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside--

speaker: interviewer
text: On the Polish side.

speaker: interviewee
text: --on the Christian side, and who is going back? So in some cases, we said, no, we're going back because we've got to pick up something. And we're going back three of us, the same which came in. So they wait another day there at the cemetery hiding somewhere and waiting till someone-- because that was very often because we did have very many casualties during the ghetto time, people which died from hunger in there, it's all written over the world. And we've been going up very often to the cemetery. Which how many can you put on a cart? Four, five people? We did have hundreds.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the carts had been going up and down all day. So they always found someone which want to stay on the Christian side, on the-- outside the ghetto. And you then took the people, and I took the cart and went inside. Because the faces haven't been important. What was important, the amount. Three people. Three people got to go in and three people got to go out. On these carts, were they always dead people or did you sometimes smuggle people out that were living?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They did always have the three people. But we did have some food which is smuggled in or things which you smuggled out to buy the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: The three people were pushing the cart. But were the so-called dead bodies, was that always dead bodies or were there sometimes a living person that you wanted to get out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't have-- not in mine. No, I didn't hear about it, no. He didn't have to do that. He could dress himself and push the wagonette by giving some money to the men which was in charge of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he didn't have to do-- but food in and things out had been easily smuggled, because the German didn't want to see it or touch it. He was scared of typhus.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you said you smuggled food. Was that all that you smuggled into the ghetto or were there other things?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We smuggled in guns for the organization. Yes. We used it too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you utilized--

speaker: interviewee
text: But mostly when we did deal-- dealt in guns and we dealt in ammunition or grenades or petrol, we always used one thing-- bribery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bribery.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We make double sure that that will go through.

speaker: interviewer
text: David--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't take any chances.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what type of bribery did you use?

speaker: interviewee
text: Money. Money or-- money, mostly money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember-- a difficult question-- do you remember how much money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we used to give, for example, 200 zloties to split between two or three people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the wage at that time, to give us a comparison, the equivalent of the 200 zloty?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zero.

speaker: interviewer
text: Zero was the wage. So that was a very big bribe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a very big bribe.

speaker: interviewer
text: David, you keep on referring to the organization, but you haven't given us a name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. ZZW, Zydowski Zwiazek Wojskowy. Betar Revisionists.

speaker: interviewer
text: Translate into English?

speaker: interviewee
text: To English. Betar, everybody knows the name. Revisionist, everybody knows the name. And the ZZW is Jewish military organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or union.

speaker: interviewee
text: Organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Organization. Thank you, David. And who was involved in this ZZW? And whilst you're looking--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Whilst you're looking for that, what was the purpose of the ZZW?

speaker: interviewee
text: The purpose of our organization?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fight against the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think you had much of a chance in fighting against the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We knew that we will lose. But at least we'll take with us as many we can, and that's a normal thing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think that the resistance is a normal thing in a fight against an enemy which did want to destroy us and to kill us. That's a normal reaction of the people which are organized and don't want to be killed by the Germans. We organized ourselves to fight them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So who headed the ZZW?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the head of the ZZW?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ZZW, that was quite a number of boys which are involved in it, quite a number of boys. I got them right in here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you friends beforehand?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you associated before the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have been never associated with the right-ist organizations, never. But I told myself that if I got to change, I will change to the one which is the more to the right than to the left, because I like to make-- I like to stop my thinking about the socialism, because I see now the fight is between Jews and Germans. And I felt that I should turn completely out of the-- helping the world or doing something for the-- to be in an organization which is together with the side-- another side. I want to be in an organization which is strictly Jewish, because Adolf didn't-- said that he's a socialist or he's a communist or he's-- Jews are Jews for him. And I told myself that's to turn is to go to the most radical organization which I can think of. The radical organization. I did it with knowing what I-- I think--

speaker: interviewer
text: You chose. You selected.

speaker: interviewee
text: I selected to go and try to be. Now, the easy part of it was that I knew even Christians, the ones which helped to organize the Jewish organization. That was the man which was very well known in the organization, in the Jewish organization, which helped him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bystry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bystry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bystry, yes. That was his code name.

speaker: interviewer
text: His code name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know his full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know his real name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did. I did, yes. I give you in a second his real name. I'm very-- this is 50 years. You ask me after 50 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know I'm asking you a very difficult question.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's a very--

speaker: interviewer
text: So why did Bystry help the Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very friendly to us, very friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a particular reason that he was--

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Iwanska.

speaker: interviewer
text: Iwanska.

speaker: interviewee
text: 'I-W-A-N-S-K-I.' Iwanska. And the code name was-- Captain, actually. He was a captain. He was with many Jews before the war in the army and the officers, cadets.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was actually in the Polish army whilst he was helping--

speaker: interviewee
text: Underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the underground.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. The start of-- by start of with a-- with a different name in the organization, the Polish organization. And then I changed, I went and I joined the AK.

speaker: interviewer
text: The AK. The A-K.

speaker: interviewee
text: The home army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the one which was the most important person was Appelbaum-- Appel-boym. That was the most important person which got a contact with Iwanska. In 1939 in October, he went already to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: October, 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He went to-- he seen the Iwanska, and he start to ask him for to help us to try-- the boys for the ZZW. At '39 already. It's a lot of talk that we start off later, but we got documentation that we start in '39. And we went right through, not very strong in '39, but slowly over the years we build it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: You became stronger.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were the first one to start the-- the underground, because the ZOB started much later. The ZOB started in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately how many of you were initially with ZZW?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean '39?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Zero. Four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Four of you. And you were one of those?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I wasn't one of them at the beginning. Not at the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was Appelbaum and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Appelbaum and another three people.

speaker: interviewer
text: All Jews. The other three were Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish people, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his contact with the Polish underground or the AK--

speaker: interviewee
text: That wasn't AK in the beginning. The organization was called differently. I think, if I'm not mistaking--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, that's OK. So what assistance did Appelbaum want from this Polish underground to assist four Jewish underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What assistance did Appelbaum expect from the Polish underground for four Jewish boys as underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: To help them, to train them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because that was the beginning. We didn't have a ghetto at that time. To train them and to give them guns and ammunitions and to help them with guns. And that was done in the beginning. I got a gift. I know that will sound very, very little. But we did have four guns in the beginning. Not we, but the four leaders did get four guns from him as a gift immediately. That was done in a manner that's-- as far as Iwanska's concerned, Iwanska did help on his own accord, not even in some cases advising his own organization. Not advising, even. Because Iwanska did give a lot to the Jewish people during the resistance all together. It's rare to mention his name, because Iwanska lost his son. He lost his brother in the fight. And he was very heavily wounded. There was a story-- I don't know how far it is true or not true. I can't say it, because I never checked it. But I know what I've been told during the resistance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was?

speaker: interviewee
text: That Iwanska's wife is either Jewish or half-Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's where his sympathy came from?

speaker: interviewee
text: That could be. I don't want to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Unconfirmed. Unconfirmed.

speaker: interviewee
text: --state it as confirmed. But I heard those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did these four Jewish partisans that began the ZZW, where did the recruitment for the numbers of the ZZW come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they start to order to get in more Betar in and everything else to the organization. But very slowly in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said these--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto-- not in the ghetto, but in the quarters, the Jewish quarters, they start to organize the Betar, and they start to do something about it to have an organization. But that took them a long time to organize it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please explain--

speaker: interviewee
text: --to put them together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you explain what a long time means in turn?

speaker: interviewee
text: A long time, I would say, took them a good one and a half year, from '39, to organize the group properly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So approximately how many members were in the group in that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been told-- I knew only about five, six. I knew much more. But to me-- to me, we'd been split in five groups of five. A leader and four. So we didn't know anything about the rest. We shouldn't know anything about the rest. But we knew--

speaker: interviewer
text: For security, you felt--

speaker: interviewee
text: For security reasons in case someone is caught.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did each group of you have a special name or a code or something?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Most probably-- yes. I haven't been any-- I've been a soldier.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a soldier. You mean you were one of the group?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the group.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you become involved with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a matter of fact, I had been involved very early in 1941 or '40, end of '40, because my wife's-- Luba's-- brother was in the organization already. And that was the friend of my friend. Then I did have two another friends, one [INAUDIBLE] Beckerman and [INAUDIBLE] Geller. But this Geller is not to be mixed up with the Geller from ZOB, because that was two Gellers. One was in our organization, one Geller was in another organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't mention the name of Luba's brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of Luba's brother?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Mayorek. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Mayorek. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Mayor.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, as your group of five, what did you do or not do? Were you constantly--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was engaged mostly, mostly, mostly, food and ammunition and guns from the Christian side, because I knew quite a lot of Christian boys. And I was not the one only, because I did have more people bringing in the guns. But I was the one which they asked to bring in guns and even machine guns.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get to know these Christian boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: From before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: You worked with them or you went to school with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school with them and I did have friends which are--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you retained that association?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did retain. I knew and the organization knew well-- and that was the most important thing-- that if I go out when the ghetto was closed or when the ghetto is open, that I can knock at 50 doors which let me in-- which will let me in-- in a way that some may not refuse to let me in. Then I will go out. But they won't harm me. No one will harm me.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say harm you, that means--

speaker: interviewee
text: Harm. To advise the Germans that I've been there or the Polish police. No one will harm me. But I got 50 places where I can knock at the door and go in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Guns and machine guns are not given for nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. We paid for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did the money come--

speaker: interviewee
text: The machine guns.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did the money come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly I bought them at the Kercelak--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Kercelak.

speaker: interviewee
text: --which was a market. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: A market.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's a very funny market there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nicholas?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: One minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: David, I'm asking you where the money came from for the guns--

speaker: interviewee
text: The money.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and you said it was in a marketplace called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You said it was in a marketplace called? For the guns, the purchase of the guns. What was the name of the marketplace?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We did-- we buy them from different places. But my place was the one which I did want to buy was Kercelak.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you translated that as a marketplace?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a market, a Polish market, which was safer for the Jews to be there than in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because mostly dealers, dealers in black market, dealers in anything you like to think of. That was a market from before the war. That was a market which was from 1900, 1900. That was a market taken by the Polish socialist ruled around there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you said the Jews were safe. But that was like an underground market?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was an-- that was like an-- no, that was a market for everybody. But underground people used it. And I explained why they used it there. There, you'd be very, very safe with the boys you dealt with. And this is the funny part of it, how that start there, how you worked there. You went into a man which called Johnny, John, Janic, or Bolic or whatever it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: You ask him, you want to buy the guns. Everything is all right. Arrange with you. Very honest. You could pay money ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Up front.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I knew that most of the boys, even that wasn't in organization, they suspect him of it. And I knew that with an organization, it's not good to do something wrong because you can be killed for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew it. So you come the next day and you're happy-- or two days later-- and you're happy with what you bought from him. And you call him again, Janic. How are you?

speaker: interviewer
text: And he looked at you. He said, what are you talking about? Who are you? I am the boy which bought from you-- which bought from you from-- you want to stop it? He wouldn't know-- he wouldn't recognize.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. He didn't want to say that is Janic. He said, what are you talking about? You are Janic? I am not Janic. I am Zbyszek. I said, but I-- he said, I'm sorry. I haven't seen you. I don't know who you are. So to walk away from him. As soon as you start to walk away, he said, but what do you want, actually? And you start again to deal with him but as a different name already. They didn't want-- they did have documents like pieces of paper, different names every day in case something happened and the Germans looked for someone, they look for man which is--

speaker: interviewer
text: The previous name.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the previous name, not the name-- so I had been very, very careful there. You could buy--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you said you could pay up front. So if they dealt in that way--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You could pay up front, to pay them front, and pick up the goods next day.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So that was fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was accepted by them. Another thing is there, which is--

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that where you bought the machine guns?

speaker: interviewee
text: The machine guns, guns, ammunition, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fine.
